# Chapter 21

[9k3m1q7v] The dust storm hit like a door slammed in the face. One mile there was sky and valley and the thin ribbon of road, the next mile there was only brown, a thickness that swallowed distance until the cars ahead were just red pinpricks trembling in murk. The world became noise: gravel under tires, wind sanding the windshield, engines straining against air that felt too heavy to breathe. Flynn’s wipers smeared grit into arcs. His implant tried to draw a clean horizon line across the glass and failed, the overlay jittering as if it was frightened too.

[2f6n8r0c] Checkpoint Three materialized out of the storm as a smear of color, banners snapping, men in goggles holding scanners tight to their chests. There was no room to slow. The road funneled through a gap between water tanks and a wrecked bus someone had dragged into place years ago. Flynn saw a figure step forward, arm out, and the arm vanished in dust as if it belonged to a ghost. The Charger’s tracker chirped green when the handheld reader caught it. The sound felt absurdly polite. He stayed in it, tires skipping over ruts, and the checkpoint disappeared behind him like something he’d imagined.

[7s1d4b9x] After the checkpoint the pack stopped being a pack and turned into scattered predators hunting by scent. The GPS line in Flynn’s dash flickered and then held, a thin cyan thread telling him where the road was supposed to be. He drove by that thread and by the faint memory of tire tracks. Every so often a car would loom out of the dust at an angle, headlights on, driver blind and stubborn, and Flynn would jink around it with inches to spare and feel his heart slam against his ribs like it wanted out.

[0v5h2c8m] Somewhere to his left a horn blared and cut off with a crunching sound that made him flinch. He did not look. Looking was how you died out here, head turned, hands still straight while the road curved away. A black shape flashed across his peripheral vision and vanished again, too fast to name. The storm erased consequences. It made every decision private, every act something you could pretend you hadn’t done because no one could swear they’d seen.

[3a8q9l1t] Cage used that privacy like a weapon. Flynn caught glimpses of the Shelby in the dust, its white stripes appearing and disappearing as if the car was teleporting. Cage would show up behind someone, close enough to read their stickers, and then press them with the nose of his car until they moved. In clear air there would have been room to yield, room to negotiate. In the storm there was only edge and void. Flynn watched a sedan drift wide and vanish with a scream of gravel, and then the Shelby slid back into the line as if nothing had happened.

[6g0p7w2j] Yuki’s RX-7 was ahead, yellow like a warning sign, its taillights blinking through grit. She held a clean line even blind, trusting the car’s lightness, trusting herself. Flynn had been chasing her since the mountain, measuring his own courage against her calm. Now he saw the Shelby’s shadow appear beside her, too close, the white stripes ghosting along her quarter panel. For a heartbeat it looked like Cage was passing. Then the Shelby’s rear stepped out on purpose and the metal kissed metal.

[1z4k6e9p] The RX-7 twitched hard to the right. Tires caught loose dirt. The car yawed and for a second it was sideways in the brown, a bright fish turning belly-up. Flynn saw Yuki’s helmeted head snap toward Cage, saw her hands fighting the wheel. There was no shoulder here, just a drop where the old road had been cut into the hillside and never properly repaired. The RX-7 slid past the last worn stripe and the world opened under it. It fell, silent at first, then tumbling, a flash of yellow rolling end over end down into nothing.

[8r2y5n0d] The explosion came late, muffled by the storm, but the light punched through the dust in a bloom of orange that turned the brown into fire for a second. Heat washed the road. Flynn tasted it, hot and oily. He heard someone on the radio say a name and then static ate the rest. Cage did not brake. The Shelby’s taillights receded, two red coals drifting deeper into the storm, and Flynn realized his hands were shaking so hard the steering wheel vibrated against his palms.

[m7c1u4h8] He kept driving because there was nothing else to do. If he stopped he would get hit from behind by someone who could not see him until impact. If he turned around he would be turning toward a ravine filled with burning fuel and broken carbon fiber. His mouth was full of dry sand. He swallowed and the grit scraped his throat. He tried to tell himself Yuki could have been revived, that a helicopter would find her, that the implants would chirp and guide rescuers. But the storm was thick enough to swallow a helicopter too. Out here technology was just another thing you brought to the desert to watch it fail.

[4t9b3s6k] Diesel’s Chevelle emerged on his left like a dark animal keeping pace. The big car was usually a steady presence, a low thunder. Now it sounded wrong. The engine note had a stutter in it, a cough that came every few seconds like someone trying to clear blood from their lungs. Diesel was pushing anyway, hood vibrating, front end light as she rode the ruts. Flynn glanced over and saw her eyes locked forward, jaw set, hands quiet on the wheel. She was climbing through the field in the storm like she could bully weather.

[q2d8m5n1] The misfire got worse. Flynn heard it even over his own engine, a metallic rattle that didn’t belong. He watched Diesel downshift and the Chevelle lurched, power lagging for half a second before it caught again. It was subtle, the kind of thing you missed if you wanted to miss it. Flynn didn’t want to miss it. A memory surfaced of Cage and Diesel in the compound, of the night before the race when Flynn had gone looking for a wrench and found Cage alone by the Chevelle with the hood up, hands deep in shadow. Cage had smiled like he’d been caught doing nothing.

[5j6x1p8f] Understanding came with a coldness that had nothing to do with wind. Cage hadn’t just been racing. He’d been pruning. Cutting away threats before the starting flag ever dropped. Flynn imagined the small, precise sabotage: a timing chain loosened, a sensor fouled, a bolt backed out until vibration did the rest. The kind of damage that waited politely until you were committed, until you were doing a hundred and eighty with your life wrapped around an engine. He looked at Diesel’s car again and saw not stubbornness but a trap closing.

[9e0c7v3a] The road straightened for a few miles and the storm thinned just enough to show the outline of the next checkpoint—tents crouched low, fuel drums lashed together, men with rags over their faces. Checkpoint Four. Three hundred and fifty miles. Flynn lifted and let the Charger drift into the marked lane. Gravel pinged the undercarriage. A man in a Don vest waved him forward with a hand that shook from cold or adrenaline. He stopped on the paint and the engine idled hard, heat rolling off the hood like breath.

[3n8f2k0w] Diesel skidded in beside him, too fast, tires biting and sliding, and the Chevelle shuddered like it wanted to tear itself apart. Diesel killed the engine and the sudden silence made the storm’s roar louder. Mechanics swarmed her car with practiced speed. One of them leaned into the bay with a flashlight and then jerked his head back, eyes wide behind goggles. Another put a hand on the valve cover as if he could feel the future through metal. Diesel stood over them, hands on hips, waiting, not asking because she already knew the question.

[c6p4t1y7] The verdict was given in gestures first. A shake of the head. A palm slicing the air. Then the mechanic shouted into the wind so Diesel could hear. “Seizure,” he said. “Soon. Catastrophic. Somebody’s done something.” Diesel’s face didn’t change. It tightened, the way a knot tightens when you pull the free end. She looked across the checkpoint and found Flynn. Dust streaked her cheeks. Her eyes were furious and tired, and in them Flynn saw the whole history of the crew, every compromise that had kept them alive.

[8u3s9q5l] Diesel mouthed words at him. The storm stole her voice but not the shape. Go. Stop him. Flynn nodded once, too fast, like obedience. He wanted to say he was sorry, that he hadn’t seen it, that he’d been a fool for thinking Cage’s cruelty was just talk. There was no time for words anyway. A fuel nozzle clanked against the Charger. The scanner chirped. Sixty seconds turned to ten. The checkpoint men were already waving him out.

[h1v7b4r9] Diesel got back into her Chevelle like she was climbing into a coffin she intended to drive. She didn’t ask the mechanics to fix it because there was no fixing, not here, not with sand in everything and a clock screaming. She fired the engine and it caught with a violent cough. Smoke puffed from the exhaust, dark and wet. She pulled out hard, the rear stepping, and for a moment Flynn thought she might stop, might choose survival over pride. Then she straightened it and disappeared into the dust with the throttle pinned.

[0m9k2d6s] The field was thinner now. Wrecks sat half-buried in sand like fossils, roofs crushed, doors torn away by scavengers who had already come for whatever was worth taking. Flynn passed a burned-out shell that might have been the RX-7, might have been some other dead car, the storm making all corpses look the same. He kept the Charger on the cyan thread. The speed climbed again because that was what the race demanded, because slowing felt like admitting you’d seen what you’d seen.

[7c5a0n8u] Fifty miles past Checkpoint Four the storm loosened enough to show shapes again—low hills, a dry wash cutting across the road, the ghost of an old billboard leaning into nothing. Flynn had just begun to think the worst of the weather was behind them when his mirror filled with Diesel’s Chevelle, closing hard, the big car swimming slightly as if the rear axle was on ice. She pulled alongside him for a second, face turned toward him, and Flynn saw she was gritting her teeth against pain or fear. The engine’s cough had become a constant rattle.

[2w6e9p1h] Then the Chevelle’s rear wheels locked. It happened without warning, no gradual fade, just an instant where the car’s posture changed and physics took over. The rear swung out. Diesel countersteered, hands fast, but a locked engine is an anchor. The tires skated across gravel. The Chevelle spun broadside at one-eighty, dust erupting from the shoulder. Flynn shouted her name into the noise like it could reach her through glass and storm.

[f4q8s2z0] The Chevelle hit a rut and lifted. The roll was slow at first, as if the car was considering whether it really wanted to die, and then it committed. Metal tore. Glass burst. The car went over and over, eight times, a dark cyclone throwing parts into the air. Flynn slammed his brakes and the Charger fishtailed, tires digging in, the seat belt biting his collarbone. Cars behind him swerved around, horns blaring, ghosting past with inches to spare. Somebody clipped his bumper and kept going.

[6x1e7k3m] Flynn killed the Charger and jumped out into wind that felt like sandpaper. The world was a roar of grit. He ran toward the wreck, boots sliding, eyes squinting against dust. The Chevelle lay upside down in the wash, roof flattened, wheels still spinning uselessly. Smoke crawled from the crushed hood. Diesel was inside somewhere. Flynn dropped to his knees and clawed at a broken window frame. The glass had already gone, leaving teeth of jagged edge.

[9p3n0t6c] He reached in and found her harness buckle by touch, fingers trembling. Diesel’s body was twisted, pinned by the collapsed roof. Her face was smeared with blood and dust, eyes half-open but not seeing. He pulled and felt something in her ribs shift with a wet sound that made him want to vomit. He did it anyway. He dragged her out over the bent metal, careful and not careful, because care was a luxury and she was losing time.

[b5r9g2v8] The medical helicopter was already coming, a dark thump growing out of the sky like a heart. The race had cameras and drones and men watching. Even in a dust storm, death was interesting. Flynn held Diesel against him on the gravel, her head lolling on his shoulder. Her implant port at the base of her neck blinked a frantic red. He could feel her breath, shallow and irregular, like a hand tapping on a door that might not open.

[1n2h8s4q] Diesel’s eyes focused for a second. She looked at him and there was no softness in it, no forgiveness, just a fierce insistence that he understand. Her lips moved. He leaned close until his cheek brushed hers and tasted salt and iron. “He,” she whispered, and the word came out as air. “Did… this.” Flynn nodded, throat tight. “I know,” he said, because lying to her now felt like another kind of violence.

[0k7c5y1d] She tried to lift a hand and couldn’t. Her gaze slid past him toward the road, toward the dust where the Shelby had vanished hours ago. “Stop,” she breathed. “Him.” Flynn felt the sentence land in him like a tool being placed in his hands. The helicopter dropped into the wash with a blast of rotor wind, medics in sealed suits running bent over. They took Diesel from him with efficient gentleness, strapping her to a board, sealing her in a cocoon of gear that hissed and blinked.

[s3v0r6a9] Flynn stood back as they loaded her into the helicopter. The rotors tore the dust into a spinning wall and then the machine lifted, climbing into brown, becoming a shadow and then nothing. He stared after it until his eyes hurt. The Charger sat on the road with its engine still ticking hot, still alive, still ready. Ahead, somewhere past the storm and the last checkpoints and the border, Cage was driving as if nothing had happened, Birdie trapped beside him, the Shelby’s taillights moving toward the finish.

[4c8m1j7n] Flynn got back into the Charger and shut the door. The cabin smelled of sweat and hot vinyl and the faint sweetness of fear. He put his hands on the wheel and felt them steady, not because he was calm but because rage can be a kind of stillness. He pictured Yuki tumbling into the ravine. He pictured Diesel’s mouth forming the word stop. He pictured Cage’s grin in the compound, the way he’d talked about consequences like they were for other people. Flynn pushed the shifter into gear. He floored it. The Charger surged forward into the dust at one-sixty, chasing a pair of red coals he intended to extinguish.
